message:
fix(aegis_entropy_anomaly): fail closed on non-finite inputs to EProcess (ft-eqtcq)

`EProcess::update_with_stream` accepts five f64 inputs — entropy,
baseline_mean, baseline_std, collapse_mean, collapse_std — and runs
them through a likelihood-ratio computation that exponentiates the
log-ratio and multiplies into `self.e_value`.

Any non-finite input propagates through the whole chain:

- `likelihood_ratio = f(entropy, baseline_*, collapse_*)` — NaN in,
  NaN out.
- `log_lr = likelihood_ratio.ln()` — NaN or -Inf.
- `exp(log_lr)` — NaN or 0.
- `self.e_value *= <NaN>` — self.e_value is now NaN permanently.

Once pinned at NaN, `is_rejected()` evaluates `NaN >= threshold`,
which is `false` for every finite threshold, so anomaly detection is
silently disabled until process restart. Same failure family as
ft-b4l62 (Histogram non-finite), ft-yskcu (continuous_backpressure
NaN cascade), ft-c17iw (semantic_anomaly non-finite embeddings),
ft-n4fdx (bayesian_ledger non-finite Evidence).

Fix: guard the top of `update_with_stream` with a composite
is_finite check on all five f64 inputs, plus a positive-std sanity
check (baseline_std > 0 and collapse_std > 0). When the check fails,
return the current `self.e_value` without updating — same
fail-closed posture the sibling beads established. The guard is the
minimum viable: five is_finite calls and two positivity checks,
cheap enough to run on every sample.

Doc comment cross-references the four sibling beads so a future
reader recovers the pattern without the tracker.

Pinning tests added under the `-- ft-eqtcq --` section (details in
the file); each test builds a calibrated EProcess, sends one or more
non-finite updates, and asserts `e_value` stays finite and
`is_rejected()` returns the expected boolean. A regression that
drops the guard flips the e_value-finite assertion to failing.

No API change. Callers that pass pre-validated finite inputs see no
behavioural difference.
